item.title: KDDI Email System Breach Exposes Up to 14.2 Million ISP Logins
Gmail Legal-Notice Phishing Uses short.gy to Hide Credential-Harvest Domain
KDDI Email Breach Exposes 14.2 Million Logins Amid Phishing Campaign Using Gmail Shortlinks for Credential Harvesting
item.content:encoded:
Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs — Must Read

Japanese telecom giant KDDI disclosed a breach exposing up to 14.2 million email logins across six ISPs after attackers exploited a vulnerability in third-party software to access account credentials, some of which were stored in plaintext.

  • KDDI Corporation discovered a breach of its email system on June 17 that also impacted five other Japanese internet service providers.
  • The attackers exploited a vulnerability in an unnamed third-party software used by KDDI.
  • Up to 14.22 million email addresses and passwords, including those of current and former customers and inactive accounts, may have been exposed.
  • Some passwords were stored in hashed or encrypted form, but KDDI did not specify what percentage of accounts had passwords stored in plaintext.
  • KDDI has been contacting affected ISPs, notified Japan's Personal Information Protection Commission and Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications, and is implementing additional security measures with the ISPs.

Legal Threat in Your Inbox: How a Gmail Shortlink Hides a Credential-Harvest Destination — Should Read

A phishing campaign used a personal Gmail account and a short.gy URL shortener to hide a credential-harvesting domain, evading authentication and URL reputation checks. Defenders must follow redirect chains fully and analyze behavioral anomalies to detect such social engineering lures.

  • The phishing email used a personal Gmail account and a bilingual legal-threat subject line to create urgency and hide the mass-BCC delivery pattern.
  • The message contained a short.gy shortened URL that resolved to a privacy-protected attacker domain (chongdaotang.net) registered in November 2023 and rated malicious.
  • All email authentication checks (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) passed because the attacker sent the message through Google’s own infrastructure, borrowing its clean reputation.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 90% confidence by analyzing the combination of a first-contact sender, URL shortener use, and high-pressure threat framing instead of relying on blacklists or authentication failures.
  • Security teams must follow shortened URLs through to their final destination during analysis, because stopping at the legitimate shortener service makes the threat invisible.

item.title: Russian SMS Phishing Targets Messaging Accounts; Clean GitHub Repos Abuse Claude Code
OpenAI GPT-5.6 Sol Preview and Healthcare Invoice Phishing via SaaS Redirects
Russian SMS Credential Theft Campaign, AI Agent Malware via Clean Repo, GPT-5.6 Sol Rollout, and Healthcare Phishing Chain
Cyera Finds Exposed Agentic AI Credentials; FBI Warns UNC5792 Targets Signal Backup Keys
